title: Harissa paste
subtitle: Tunisian hot chilli and spice paste
excerpt: The foundational condiment of Tunisian cooking: dried red chillies blended with garlic, caraway, coriander, cumin, and olive oil into a fiery, fragrant paste. Used as a condiment, stirred into braises, spread on bread, and added to everything. The heat level is adjustable; the flavour is not substitutable.
sourceNotes: Harissa is the defining condiment of Tunisian cooking and is used across the Maghreb, though Tunisia is its home. Commercial harissa (Cap Bon brand is the reference standard) is widely available, but homemade produces a fresher, more complex flavour. The caraway seed distinguishes Tunisian harissa from Moroccan-style chilli pastes.

content.text: The heat level of homemade harissa depends entirely on the dried chillies used. Dried Tunisian chillies (available in North African shops) produce the authentic result. A mix of dried guajillo and dried cayenne works well as a substitute: more guajillo for milder, more complex flavour; more cayenne for heat. Remove the seeds before soaking to reduce heat.

Caraway is the spice that distinguishes harissa from other chilli pastes. Its aniseed note is subtle in the finished paste but unmistakeable. Do not substitute fennel seeds, the flavour is different. Soak the dried chillies in boiling water for at least 20 minutes until completely softened before blending.

Remove seeds from the {{dried-chilli}} for a milder result, or leave them for full heat. Cover the chillies in boiling water and soak for 20 to 30 minutes until completely soft. Drain, reserving 2 tablespoons of the soaking water. Toast the {{caraway-seeds}} in a dry pan for 1 minute until fragrant. Combine the soaked chillies, {{garlic}}, toasted caraway, {{coriander-ground}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{lemon-juice}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and the reserved soaking water in a blender. Blitz to a smooth paste. Add the {{olive-oil}} and blend again until emulsified. Taste and adjust salt and lemon. Transfer to a jar and pour a thin layer of olive oil over the surface to preserve it.

items.fix: Remove all seeds before soaking to reduce heat significantly. Once made, dilute with more olive oil and add a roasted red pepper to mellow and sweeten
items.cause: Seeds not removed or very hot chilli variety used
items.symptom: Harissa too hot to use as a condiment

items.fix: Soak for a full 30 minutes minimum. The skins must be completely soft before blending. Strain through a sieve after blending if texture remains coarse
Toast the caraway seeds until fragrant before adding. The lemon juice is essential, add more if the paste tastes flat
items.cause: Caraway not toasted or too little acid

content.text: Harissa is on every Tunisian table, served as a condiment alongside every meal. In Tunisia it is stirred into braises and soups, spread on bread at breakfast, and mixed with olive oil as a dipping sauce. Across the Maghreb it appears in Moroccan and Algerian cooking as an import, though the Tunisian version with caraway is distinct. Globally, harissa has become one of the most popular North African exports: it appears in supermarkets and restaurant kitchens worldwide, used far beyond its original context. Homemade remains significantly better than all but the best commercial versions.
